speaker: interviewer
text: --on tape 2 with Mr. Emil Jacoby, also known as Jacobovitz. Today is December 18, 1995. Mr. Jacobovitz, you were talking about the junior high school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in-- I can't pronounce it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chust, Chust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chust, OK. We were talking about the junior high school before Chust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So a junior high--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- was it a-- it was a secular school, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Secular school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get along with the non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any anti-Semitic--

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: --incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no incident in-- no incidents in school. But outside of the school, when I came home-- at that time, even before this crisis of, of Munich in 1938-- it started already in 1934, after Hitler came to power. I noticed, when I went to visit my grandma, which is in Terebla-- she lived with my-- with my sister. They were under a-- also a grocery and a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] drinking-- serving drinking for people there, alcohol, wine. When I was on my bike-- I was riding there. When I came to that village inn. In our town, Bustino, I didn't hear no, no, not-- at that time, I came, and they-- I was there as a Jew. I, I dressed as a Jew. I looked like a Jew. The kids would run from their houses to the-- to the fence and see who is passing by-- oh! And they start screaming at me and throwing stones and rocks on me and slur-- Jewish slurs, like, Jew, go to Palestine, and all kind of slurs-- 'dirty Jew.' I would hear already at that time from 1935, 1936, 1937, 1938. But in our school, we were only Czech, Czech people. In this area was many ethnic groups where I was part of that area. There were Ruthenians, Hungarians, Czechs, and also Romanians, and Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to a Jewish Czech school. There were also Hungarian schools and the Ruthenian schools. Many Jewish people entered other schools. There were-- all schools acknowledged by the government, Czech government, as legal schools for education. But my father sent me to a Czech school. And these boys and girls in the Czech school, we were-- Jewish people were more-- were the majority of the school, see? There were-- there were not so many Czech boys in school-- and girls who went to the school because they came only-- they were sent from Czech Republic to this area, to this area [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to do a job, like, like the state employee be, or working on the train, working in the post office, like these area-- these jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were not-- it's the Czech people are not-- very rarely when somebody is anti-Semite. Maybe he is in his heart, but he doesn't show it. So I didn't know-- and never encountered any Jewish anti-Semitism from a Czech person, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your hobbies as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: For hobbies, I was-- draw my-- to, to draw people, to draw faces, to draw all kinds of things, animals. I liked to draw with a pencil on a paper. Once I draw the, the portrait of president-- our president, Masaryk. And the teacher send this portrait to the president, to Prague. And I got a letter from him that he was very happy to-- that such talented kids in, in-- And I have to mention, also, that this area where I'm from is a unique place on the map-- on the European map. This is not a big area. It's the size of, like, New Jersey, let's say. And it was-- it's changed hands-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I don't know many-- how many-- how many times. It was under occupation of the Romans. The Romans were there. Then there were the Turkish Empire, the Turks-- under the Turks. And then came the, the Russians in World War I. And then it was the Austrian-Hungarian Empire. It belonged to the-- part of that empire was this area, this territory. And then in World War II, again-- it was between World War I and World War II the Czech Republic was established. And this territory was, was annexed to the Czech Republic. So we were part of Czechoslovakia when I was born. And during World War II, Hungary occupied this territory-- the Hungarian army. After World War II, again, Soviet Union-- it was attached to Soviet Union-- was part of Soviet Union. And now, it is part of Ukraine. Ukraine, she split with Soviet Union. She became independent. And she took this territory. And, and she was-- and she's-- this is part of the Ukraine. So it's, it's, it's-- it's a very-- many, many ethnic groups are there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in the Czech school. I happened to go to the Czech school. And there was no anti-Semitism. When did things start to change? When did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The anti-Semitism? I told you that I noticed it already after the-- Hitler came to the power when I went to village. In, in our-- in our town, there was no big anti-Semite expressions because-- I don't know why. There was-- the people were more, more city-like. They were not coming in a village, like I went to Tereblya. There-- and when a Jew went on the street, the whole village was with their eyes like this. And they probably were more anti-Jewish because of Jewish merchants. They were very-- at that time, they had the-- they had some, some kind of complaints against Jewish merchants. And this complaint was, was-- made, made sense. Because I happen to remember, when I used to help out my father in store-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he told me on a Thursday he needed help because Thursday was the market day-- for the market day. And all these-- in-- around the surrounding villages and, and-- came to shop and to sell some stuff-- and to shop. At that time, they would stop and buy all kind of-- so they would buy, say, a pound of sugar or a pound of, of something else, rice. And we didn't have these packages. We had to weigh it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So instead of weighing, to go faster, you would prepare, say, like, 20 or 30 bags. And then somebody would come and stand-- they were standing in line. We would just give them the, the bag of sugar and take the money. And this will go faster. So I-- he would put me near the-- near the-- to weigh the sugar. And you would have to put on one side a weight of, say, a pound, and on the other side, to put-- they pay for the bag, to open it, and start to pour in the sugar, right? So I would do it, looking how he's doing it, right? And then he would say, no, no, no, don't do it this way. Why not? He says, look at me. You don't put the bag slowly down. You drop it in order for the-- for the-- Scale.

speaker: interviewee
text: --scale to move. This way, you don't have to put so much stuff inside-- when the scale is moving. So I did it this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I told him, Abba, it's not fair. This way, you're giving for-- less than, than, than the guy's paying for it. He pays for one pound. He wants to get a pound of, of sugar, all right? And when you do it this way, you make the scale moving, he wouldn't get exactly a pound.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, this is-- this is a gimmick. You don't understand. And we start an argument about it. He would make me-- get out, you don't want to do it, then don't do it. I said, I want to do it, but I want-- I want you to be fair, not to do it this way. Do it my way.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you do it my way. Because, he says, if I want to have the, the, the price of the bag-- I want to have it-- not only a profit on the rice-- he will teach me a lesson. He says, when you come and your-- and your mother sends you for the-- for, for rice, you give money? I said, no. He said, who is paying for the rice when I give her-- rice for your mother in the kitchen? We have to get this from someone somewhere, he says. The profit is-- the profit is, is, is OK. But I have to also to get paid for the bag, which I am giving this stuff inside, you know, and also for the rice which I consume. So this-- the, the, the, the, the residents, the Ruthenians, the non-Jewish people who would come to shop, they would go home. And they would say that it's not a full pound that they bought. And they would get mad at the Jewish storekeepers. And they would-- that's why they start cursing at them. It's only a small thing, right? But this, together with the-- with the priest in, in the church, when they go and he gives a sermon, and he mentions them that Jesus was killed by the Jews, they created a climate of anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then Hitler came to power. Oh, Hitler is coming! they would cry. They would scream at me, Hitler is coming, you dirty Jew! You go to Palestine, not steal here in, in our-- in, in, in, in, in here steal from us. We were considered foreigners there, even though my father and my mother, they lived there for generations and generations in this-- in this-- in this area. But they never considered us as full citizens, recognizing us as citizens over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things change for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, the Holocaust actually began in 1938, when I was shut out of-- of the school, when they said, no more school due to the-- due to the mobilization. And since then-- since then, they wouldn't let us back in the school. They would let us go to Hungarian schools, not to Czech schools. Because the Czechs themselves couldn't go to school. It's not only Jewish people. They closed the schools. So Jewish used to go-- would go to Hungarian schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a Hungarian--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't, no. Because I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to hear your story, not--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't speak-- I didn't know how to speak Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was hanging, hanging out with other kids. And I, I, I-- idle all the time, sometimes helping my, my father in store on a Thursday. But most of the time, I didn't have much work to do. I would read books. I would go out to see a movie with other guys. Playing soccer was my favorite game. And in winter time, I would-- ice skating. I would-- in summer, I would go swimming, also, with, with other kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I behaved like a normal-- like other kids. But my father didn't like it because I started to play cards, too-- with other kids playing cards. And my mother would give me-- my father wouldn't give me money. He would-- he would-- and my mother would give me sometimes money to go and play cards with all the boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when he-- when he-- once, he caught me playing cards. He give me the-- he beat the hell out of me. So I wouldn't-- I didn't like it. I said, oh, if I cannot stay home and do my, my things which I like to do, then I-- I'm going to have to look somewhere else to go and learn some kind of a skill, some trade.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I heard that my-- it was in 1938-- my-- like, in autumn, the end of summer, I heard that a cousin of mine, he went to Brunn. Brunn is the capital of Moravia. A province of Czechoslovakia called Moravia-- and the capital of that province is called Brunn, or Brno in Czech.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there-- his mother told me that [? Fred ?] is in Brunn. So I said to my mother, I'm going to Brunn-- to [? Fred. ?] Maybe I'll do what he's doing. And one day, I went with my friends to see a movie. And I didn't tell my mother that I'm going. I told her I would like to go. But I didn't tell her when and, and, and how. And I just-- I said-- talked to my friends. I'm going to, to Brunn to see [? Fred. ?] And my friends, [? Bela ?] and [? Fred, ?] they were my best friends. We were like buddies all the-- all the time, pals. We went to the same class-- school buddies. And they come to say goodbye to me to the train station. And I said, goodbye. I'm going. I just took a, a small bundle of things from the-- when we saw the movie was over, they showed us-- showed me off to the train. I, I boarded the train. I bought a ticket-- Bustino to Brunn. It took me a whole day go get there, because the train didn't run properly. It was all kind of-- had to stop over and wait for another train. So it took me almost-- more than a day to get to Brunn. I came to Brunn. I had never been in a big city. I didn't know where, where he is. I don't know his address. I was lost in this city.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, I went-- I found a Jewish community. I saw written, 'Jewish Community.' And I was asking-- yeah, I, I remembered the name of the place were [? Fred's ?] supposed to work. They called it 'Stiassni.' Stiassni was the biggest textile factory in Czechoslovakia, the biggest. So finally I got to the Stiassni office. And I was asking for a job. I said-- I, I had the school clothes. They don't accept no more us in school. And I am from Bustino, from the-- Carpathia. And I would like to work something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I showed them some sketches. I brought with me also sketch-- what I am sketching. Maybe I can do something which in textiles they use for, for sketching. He said, OK, we will-- we will give you a job. He give me a job. And when I went to the-- to the cafeteria to have lunch, I, I met my friend [? Freddie ?] over there. He was very happy. And he took me to his-- where he lives, where he rented a room. And I rented a bed in, in, in the same room he was sleeping with this [? Fred. ?] And we were very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed not long because I was-- I wanted to save my own place or to have a room-and-board place. It wasn't-- it was not clean. It was not so clean over there, I remember, the whole thing. And so I went to the Jewish community. And they give me an address where I, I got a place with room and board in Brno. And I kept working. And I met [? Freddie ?] always after work. And we got together. It was good. I was very happy. And I took a picture. I wrote my parents home a letter that I'm working with [? Freddie. ?] And they were happy. And I bought myself a new dress-- a new suit, jacket and pants. And everything was fine. All of a sudden, I came home-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, I got up all of a sudden in the morning to work, and I thought I'm dreaming. I see big Nazi, Nazi-- it was already in '39. That was-- I started in '38-- at the end of '38. And it was in March, '39, right, I went to work. And the whole city was full of flags, Nazi, with the swastikas. And I, I go further. I see Jewish stores broken-- the windows, and all kind of paint written, 'Juden nach Dachau,' 'the Jews to Dachau.' And I kept going. I see a Jewish synagogue burned, burned. And I went straight to where I was working, my working place-- closed, closed. It was a Jewish, Jewish owner.

speaker: interviewee
text: What am I supposed to do? I started running in the streets. Jew-- and then came marching German troops marching on the streets, singing. And on the-- on the sidewalk, there was working a lot of officers going to shop and stop to look at the windows. All the Jewish windows were broken. And then I came to the main square of Brno. And there was a, a big crowd. What is going on here? I'm waiting, see what is coming up. And they're looking all to this huge building, which is supposed to be the city hall or something, with the balconies. And there are flags over there, and music, and they're clapping. All of a sudden comes out a person on the- on the-- on the balcony. And I look good. And I say, is this Hitler or not? And I ask somebody, who is that? They said, this is Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they start going like this, all the people. And they look at me why, why I'm not going like this, saluting Hitler. And they are so proud, clapping hands, and that's it. And he went for-- only for a few seconds. He showed-- he come-- he was going, saluting the people. And he went inside. But for a few seconds, I saw him. And everybody said, it's him, with, with his mustache, with his-- like I saw on the pictures many times. He came to, to say hi to the people over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I kept going and going until I reached-- I came back home. It was already evening. The whole day I was going around in circles, looking and thinking what is going to happen. When I came home, I found there with this superintendent a note for me-- on my name, a note. From where? From the Gestapo. I have to report tomorrow morning this and this hour to the headquarters of the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there-- in the next morning I came there. And I came inside to the guard. And he directed me where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went up the stairs. I came inside to a-- another-- to a desk. And I showed him my, my note. And he says, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wait here. I waiting. Comes another Gestapo, takes me-- gives me a, a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some kind of a bucket. Take water, take this mat, and start cleaning the whole-- all around, it was a big building-- all these rooms over there, when you come in, the halls, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hallway.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the halls where it's stones. And I had to wash it and clean it. And when you finish, you come to me. OK, It took me about a few hours to finish it. I came back. And he says, OK, now go on this line over there. There was a line of people like myself, waiting in line. When my turn came, I got inside an office. I saw there a Gestapo sitting behind the desk. I give him the-- my note. He asked me name-- the name, where I was born-- all this information he wrote down.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you want to go to work? That's what he said. You want to go to work? Or you want to go home? I said, work? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the difference? You want to go to work or go home? I would like to work. But I have parents. And I want to call them or let them know where I'm going to work because they are-- I said, they are worried about me. They would know. OK, he said, this is-- Lublin is the name, Lublin. Do you know what that is? I never heard of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin is a place. We are there open for, for, for Jewish people. They have their own place. There is a, a Jewish council named the Judenrat. They will take care of everything. You can live there and work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no, I would like to go home first, and then, maybe later I will go to work. OK, go. He give me the seal. He signed-- to go. Where do I go? he said, go ask over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ask the man at the outside desk. He says, tomorrow morning, this-and-this hour, you report at the station and this-and-this place. There will be many over there like you. A train will take you home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next day I came. It was like he said. There  was a lot of Jewish people. One would go to all over-- in our area, different places. And they were all from my-- from my province, this Podkarpatska Rus-- 'Karpatska Rus,' they call it. So I'm waiting. A train comes. This Gestapo puts out-- put us in a train, in a-- not in a cattle train, a normal train, a passenger train. Very nice-- we are going with this train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is one-- one Gestapo was, was going with the-- with the-- with the whole group together with us. So we reached the border of Czechia and Slovakia, you know. Czechoslovakia, there was one country. But then when Hitler occupied Brno and the whole Czech area, he gave for Slovakia to have their own independent country. Because the, the-- there was a Nazi leadership who, who were in contact with the-- with the German-- with the Nazis. And, and since they promised to back up the Hitler's policies and everything, carry out anti-Jewish policies and this, they give us, the Slovaks, to have their own-- their own government, their own state. And this Tiso was-- he was a-- was a priest. And he was named the, the minister or president of that country. And Hlinka was his-- the head of the Nazi party in Slovakia. They called it the Hlinka Guard-- Hlinka Guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we reach the stop between-- on the border between Czech, Moravia, and Slovakia, this Gestapo which he came with us, he handed over a paper to the Hlinka Guard who took-- he told him to take over these people. And it's all-- you are-- they are yours. And he went back to Brunn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we are under the-- under the orders of this Hlinka Guard, these Nazi troops. And we didn't suspect anything bad. But then we, we, we-- they, they licked us very bad. They put us-- they took us down with-- in a rude manner, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yelling and screaming and kicking us, and put us on a-- on a truck. And they took us to Kosice. It was a big city, Kosice, in, in, in, what, eastern Slovakia. In that city, they took us also to a military camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it? And we're going to pause again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
